title-group.article-title: Relationship Between Central Sensitization and Genetic Polymorphisms—A Case–Control Study in Fibromyalgia

aff.named-content: Direction de la Recherche Clinique et Des Innovations
Division of Clinical Pharmacology and Toxicology, Department of Anesthesiology, Pharmacology, Intensive Care, and Emergency Medicine

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Fibromyalgia is a syndrome characterised by widespread chronic pain, which is believed to be mediated by a state of central sensitisation (CS). Based on the hypothesis that CS itself could have genetic determinants, our aim was to further explore this pathway.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This study included 201 women with fibromyalgia and 40 healthy women matched on age and menopausal status. The nociceptive flexion reflex (NFR) threshold was used as a surrogate marker of CS. Genotyping of 60 candidate‐genes likely to influence the pathogenesis of fibromyalgia or chronic pain was explored in all subjects.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The NFR threshold was lower in patients (n = 141, median and [interquartile range]: 34 mA [24–46]) than in controls (n = 35, 58 mA [44–77], p = 0.0003), but none of the tested polymorphism genotype was significant. An association was found between fibromyalgia and three polymorphisms related to TNF, TACR1, and IL10 genes, either by testing the simple allelic frequency or an additive model. When the analysis was adjusted on NFR threshold, only the association with TACR1 was still significant, along with other associations with polymorphisms related to FAAH, HTR1A, and MYT1L. Associations were however non significant when a Bonferroni's correction was applied.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study confirmed that CS is a major feature of fibromyalgia. Although several polymorphisms stand out in the analysis, no significant relationship between CS and 60 selected candidate‐genes genotype could be highlighted. This information may help however to guide further research to explore this important pathway in larger well phenotyped cohorts.

sec.title: Significance Statement
sec.p: Sixty selected fibromyalgia and pain candidate‐genes have no significant association with the nociceptive flexion reflex, a surrogate marker of central sensitization in 201 fibromyalgia women. But the study may help however to guide further research to explore this important pathway in larger well phenotyped cohorts.

sec.title: Trial Registration
sec.p: ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT04624581

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The fibromyalgia syndrome (FMS) is a chronic pain condition with a worldwide prevalence of 2% to 4% (Berwick et al. 2022). It associates chronic widespread pain (CWP) with sleep disturbances, fatigue, impaired cognitive and physical function, psychological distress, and other comorbidities (Giorgi et al. 2022; Nicholas et al. 2019). CWP is defined by the IASP as nociplastic pain, that is, “pain that arises from altered nociception despite no clear evidence of actual or threatened tissue damage causing the activation of peripheral nociceptors or evidence for disease or lesion of the somatosensory system causing the pain” (Kosek et al. 2016; Raja et al. 2020). Presentations of FMS are heterogeneous and classifications according to different symptomatic, biological, psychological or sociological features, do not reach consensus (Yim et al. 2017). Moreover, the underlying mechanisms of CWP are still not clear, although a pivotal role for central sensitisation (CS), an “amplification of neural signalling within the central nervous system that elicits pain hypersensitivity” is underlined (Woolf 2011). Abnormal cortical processing, increased activity in the pain matrix, alterations of the inhibitory pain modulation and molecular changes in pain pathways have been described in FMS (Gyorfi et al. 2022).

Quantification of CS seems to be possible in humans by exploring the nociceptive flexion reflex (NFR), a spinal polysynaptic withdrawal reflex (Skljarevski and Ramadan 2002) and a validated measurement of spinal nociceptive excitability (Leone et al. 2025). The NFR threshold, that is, the lowest noxious stimulation intensity required to trigger a reflex motor response (Leroux et al. 1995), is decreased in patients with chronic musculoskeletal pain. It has been suggested as a good surrogate of CS, as shown in 5 of the 9 included studies of a recent systematic review, despite methodological disparities (Lim et al. 2011).

A genetic predisposition is also highly suspected in FMS. A review of seven cohort studies of twins reported heritability rates from 27% to 71% for CWP (Kerr and Burri 2017), and familial aggregation in FMS patients (Hackshaw 2021). Many candidate‐gene association studies have been conducted in the last three decades and these have identified more than 50 genes associated with FMS, including monoamines, catechol‐O‐methyl‐transferase (COMT), and neurotransmitters involved in pain (Kerr and Burri 2017; D'Agnelli et al. 2019; Janssen et al. 2021). Genome‐wide association studies (GWAS) have also been conducted in CWP/FMS, but none of the significantly associated single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) provided a definite determinant of the hypothetic pathophysiology (Docampo et al. 2014; Rahman et al. 2021).

As CS appears as a major feature of CWP, our hypothesis is that it could be a mediator of the effect of the genotype on the FMS phenotype. In line with this, in FMS patients, the NFR threshold has been reported to be influenced by a variant of COMT, a gene often cited in the pathogenesis of FMS (Desmeules et al. 2014), and by a variant possibly linked to the synthesis of a neuronal trafficking protein (Gloor et al. 2022). Then, the objective of the current study was to further explore the mediating role of CS in the genome‐related pathogenesis of FMS, this by testing a more refined set of candidate genes. With the same methods as in the two above‐mentioned studies, we used the NFR threshold as the surrogate marker of CS.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: This prospective case–control study was conducted according to the guidelines of the Declaration of Helsinki, was approved by a research Ethics committee (CPP Ile‐de‐France II, Paris, France) on 31/08/2020 (20.06.30.58854 RIPH2 HPS) and registered on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT04624581). The study took place at the Clinical Investigation Centre of the University Hospital of Clermont‐Ferrand (France).

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: All participants were women, 18 to 65 years old, and gave their informed consent prior to the inclusion. The FMS cases were defined using the 2016 American College of Rheumatology criteria of FMS (Wolfe et al. 2016). The healthy volunteers were matched on age (±5 years) and menopausal status with FMS patients and must be free from any chronic or current pain or analgesic medication.

Exclusion criteria for all participant were: childbearing age with no effective contraceptive, pregnancy or breastfeeding; concomitant involvement in another interventional trial; antibiotics intake in the last 3 months, any serious disease that could interfere with the study. FMS patient with long‐term drug treatment could be included but had to accept to be free of any analgesics medication the day of the visit for the exploration.

sec.title: Central Sensitization Test
sec.p: Two objective parameters have been measured to assess CS in FMS cases and healthy volunteers: the NFR threshold (primary parameter), and the temporal summation (TS). The Central Sensitization Inventory (CSI) Part A questionnaire‐based assessment of CS was also obtained.

NFR was explored as previously described (Desmeules et al. 2003): we used a Nicolet Viking Quest 2 CH device (Nicolet Biomedical, Madison, WI, USA). The participant was lying in bed in a supine position, in order to obtain muscular relaxation. Electromyographic responses were recorded using a pair of surface electrodes (single differential), placed over the biceps femoris muscle of the dominant side, 10 cm above the popliteal fossa, with a reference electrode attached over the anterolateral aspect of the ipsilateral tibia bone plateau. Repeated electrocutaneous stimulations were applied over the retro‐malleolar track of the ipsilateral sural nerve using a pair of electrodes, and delivered with a single rectangular impulse (0.5 ms) and a 6–10 s interstimulus interval, by a constant current stimulator at intensities ranging from 1 to 100 mA. The NFR was identified as a multiphasic signal appearing with a latency between 90 and 250 ms, and was considered to be present when the corrected computed surface was 0.5 mV.ms−1 (positive response). The participant was instructed that the sensation intensity could randomly increase, decrease, or stay the same, with stimulus repetition occurring independently of her reply. Following every electrical stimulation of the sural nerve, the participant was asked to rate her pain on a numerical rating scale (NRS) from 0 (no pain at all) to 10 (worst pain imaginable) with a positively painful response defined by a cut‐off value of 4. A successful NFR threshold was then defined as the intensity of current (in mA) that induced 50% of positive responses to a series of 30 to 40 stimulations, by fitting the percentage of positive responses to Hill's equation. We can specify that increasing the range does increase the number of possible stimulations, but not this of painful ones, as this was an ascending method with an up‐and‐down adjustment made only around the first level of painful intensities, so it was generally possible to estimate the threshold after 10 painful stimulations or less, whatever the threshold. Evoked fear or anxiety was avoided by the choice made in our protocol not to increase intensity and we stopped the test when the patient felt the electrical stimulation as too painful, even before the NFR had been evoked.

There is no gold standard to assess NFR with sometimes heterogeneity in the results (Amiri et al. 2021), our choice was to follow a methodology already used (Desmeules et al. 2003), not only because it succeeded to identify a greater CS in FMS (which is our working hypothesis), but also it identified interactions between genetic variants and CS in FMS (Gloor et al. 2022). The investigators in charge of explorations (V.L. and N.M.) have been trained to the methodology in Geneva University Hospital. The measurement of the NFR threshold could not be obtained for all individuals, because the electrical stimulation was too painful (“early pain failure”) or because no multiphasic signal appeared even after strong stimulations or once the cut‐off level of 100 mA stimulation was reached (“excessive threshold failure”). In both situations, stop values were recorded.

TS was measured by using a size 6.45 (180‐g) Von Frey hair applied to the volar side of the non‐dominant forearm. The hair was applied once, and the participant rated her pain as described previously, then the hair was applied 10 times at a rate of 1 Hz and pain was rated during this series. This exploration was repeated 5 times. TS was calculated as the ratio of pain elicited by the series of 10 stimuli and by the initial single stimulus; the higher the ratio, the higher TS (Latremoliere and Woolf 2009).

The CSI test Part A (Mayer et al. 2012) assesses the frequency of health‐related symptoms associated with CS syndromes. The participant was asked to rate each question of the 25‐item survey on a 5‐point scale (0 = “never”, 4 = “always”) with a maximum sum of 100. Higher CSI scores represent greater self‐reported symptomatology. A cut‐off score of 40 or greater has been shown as acceptable in psychometrics for identifying patients with CS syndromes (Neblett et al. 2013; Salaffi et al. 2022).

sec.title: Genomics
sec.p: A 4 mL blood sample was collected in an EDTA tube for every individual. The tubes were aliquoted in 1 mL of whole blood and stored frozen at −80°C until processing by the Laboratory for Clinical Pharmacology and Toxicology of the Geneva University Hospital (HUG). DNA purification and concentration of 241 samples (201 FMS patients and 40 controls) were performed using the QIAsymphony DSP DNA kit (QIAGEN) on a QIAsymphony SP/AS. Quantification was performed with the Invitrogen BR dsDNA Assay kit on a Qubit 4 Fluorometer (both from Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, United States of America).

A total of 60 SNPs of 48 candidate genes (Table 1) were selected from the literature (44 SNPs) and from HUG previous study (Gloor et al. 2022). SNP selection was based on the strategy outlined by Belfer et al. (Belfer et al. 2004), taking into account: (i) demonstrated clinical association, (ii) clinical study replication, (iii) predicted polymorphism abundance in the target population, and (iv) SNP functionality (Tunbridge et al. 2019).

caption.p: List of the genes and SNPs tested in the current study.

fn.p: Note: For each gene or region listed in alphabetic order are shown, from left to right: the chromosome where the gene or region is located (“Chr”); the single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) that has been tested; the name of the protein encoded by the gene, if known (“rec” stands for “receptor”); the function of the protein within either the pathophysiology of pain or of fibromyalgia syndrome (FMS), if known; the type of previously reported association between the gene or region, and conditions close to fibromyalgia, if any; and the literature references.

Abbreviations: antinoc, antinociception; BMS, burning mouth syndrome; CS, central sensitisation; CWP, chronic widespread pain; desc, descending; exp, experimental; FMS, fibromyalgia syndrome; GWAS, genome‐wide association study; IBS, irritable bowel syndrome; ns, non‐significant; OA, osteoarthritis; periph, peripheral; regen, regeneration; regul, regulation; S.sp, supraspinal; s/u, subunit; sensit, sensitization; Sp, spinal; TMD, temporomandibular disorder; transd, transduction.

sec.p: The cut‐off for minor allele frequency (MAF) was > 0.1. All genetic coordinates refer to the Ensembl GRCh37 reference assembly. After selecting the 60 targets SNPs, a custom TaqMan OpenArray Genotyping array has been prepared by Life Technologies (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, United States of America). Genotyping was performed at the iGE3 genomic platform of the University of Geneva (UniGE) using an Open Array AccuFill System and a QuantStudio 12 K Flex real‐time PCR system from Life Technologies.

sec.title: FMS Pain and Impact Status
sec.p: FMS pain and impact status assessments were done in FMS patients only. CWP, as a recall of the 2 past weeks, was assessed by the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), a 11‐item questionnaire. The response to each item on a 11‐point NRS and two scores ranging from 0 to 10 are calculated, one for pain severity (i.e., overall averaged intensity), and one for pain interference (i.e., overall averaged impact of pain on different physical and psychosocial aspects of daily life Cleeland and Ryan 1994). Another aspect of the impact of FMS was assessed using the Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire (FIQ); an average impact of FMS is defined with FIQ over > 50, and a severe impact with FIC over 70 (Bennett 2009).

sec.title: Sample Size Calculation and Statistical Analyses
sec.p: The sample size was estimated firstly in order to detect differences between patients and controls for the NFR threshold. We based our calculation on the study of Desmeules et al. that compared a mean NFR threshold of 22.7 mA in the 85 FMS cases to 33 mA in the 40 matched controls (Desmeules et al. 2003). With α = 5% and 1–β = 95%, we estimated that 100 cases and 20 controls were enough to identify a difference between cases and controls. However, by simulating effect size on this basis, we estimated that, with 200 fibromyalgia patients and 40 healthy volunteers, an effect size greater than 0.5 can be highlighted with a two‐sided type I error of 5% and a statistical power greater than 80%. This sample size is also sufficient to detect differences in allelic frequency between groups, assuming odds ratios greater than 2 and the same statistical assumptions parameters. The sample size calculation was performed on Stata.

Continuous data were expressed as mean and standard deviation or as quartiles, depending on their distribution. The assumption of normality (Gaussian distribution) was assessed using the Shapiro–Wilk test. In order to study the genetic signature of FMS, we used a stepwise approach from the most simple test of association between the genotype (gene by gene) and the main phenotype (case/control, i.e., FMS yes/no), to analysis of the mediation by central sensitisation on this association, if any. To do so, we used two different ways, (i) by studying the relationship between the genotype and the without and with adjustment on the NFR threshold, and (ii) by studying the relationship between the genotype and the NFR threshold, here considered as an “intermediate” phenotype (mediator). A mediating effect would be suggested by either a stronger significance obtained after adjustment or a direct relationship. Comparisons between groups (patients vs. controls) were performed using the chi‐square test or the Fisher's exact test for categorical variables, and Student's t‐test or Mann–Whitney U test for continuous variables. Allelic frequency comparisons were conducted using the chi‐square test or the Fisher's exact test, as well as logistic regression and Wright's fixation index analysis. A complementary analysis was conducted according to an additive genetic model, in which the dependent variable was no longer the minor allele frequency but a score attributed to each observation, with 0 for the genotype ‘AA’, 1 for ‘Aa’, and 2 for ‘aa’. The score was then tested with the phenotype (case/control) as the sole explanatory variable (unadjusted logistic regression), and with the NFR threshold as a covariate (adjusted logistic regression). Results were presented as odds ratios (OR) with their 95% confidence intervals. The additive score was also tested with the NFR threshold as the sole explanatory variable within the sample of FMS cases, by using a linear regression. A linear regression was also used to test the correlations between MAFs, of the study (study controls vs. expected European population and cases vs. controls); the significance of the relationship was tested by an ANOVA and the effect size by the coefficient of determination R 2. Statistical analyses were performed using Stata software (version 15, StataCorp, College Station, TX, USA) and XLStat (Addinsoft, Paris, F). A two‐sided type I error rate of 5% was considered statistically significant. The p‐values were directly interpreted without and with a Bonferroni's correction for multiple comparisons.

The data presented in this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to containing information that could compromise the privacy of research participants.

sec.title: Sample Description
sec.p: Participants were included from November 2020 to January 2022. The flow chart of the study is shown in Figure 1. Patients and controls were matched based on age, BMI, and menopause status. All FMS patients were positive for the ACR 2016 criteria (Table 2). They also reported high pain severity and interference scores (BPI), with an important impact of FMS as evaluated by the FIQ, some patients being severely affected (FIQ > 70).

caption.p: STROBE flow chart of the study.

Abbreviations: ACR, American College of Rheumatology; BMI, body mass index; BPI, brief pain inventory; CS, component summary (quality of life); CSI, central sensitization inventory; FIQ, fibromyalgia impact questionnaire (total score); FMS, fibromyalgia syndrome; FS, fibromyalgia score; NA, not applicable; NC, not calculated; SSS, symptom severity score; WPI, widespread pain index.

sec.title: CS Testing
sec.p: Results of CS are described in Table 3. NFR threshold was obtained in 70.1% of FMS cases and 87.5% controls. Compared to controls, FMS patients had a greater CS, lower threshold values (p = 0.0003), a higher pain score when NFR threshold was reached (p = 0.002), a higher TS ratio (p < 0.0001), and a higher CSI total score (p < 0.0001).

fn.p: Note: The numerical values are expressed as median and [First quartile–Third quartile]; the categorical values are expressed as headcount and (percentage of the related sample).
Abbreviations: CSI, central sensitization inventory; FMS, fibromyalgia syndrome; NFR, nociceptive flexion reflex.

sec.title: Genetic Association Analyses With Disease
sec.p: All samples and SNPs were above the call rate (CR) threshold (> 98%) and respected the Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium (p‐value threshold < 1.10−3 (0.000833 = 0.05/60)). The mean CR (SNP and sample) was 99.84%. The MAF observed in the sample of controls was well correlated with that expected in the European population (as reported by https://www.ensembl.org/index.html), with an R2 of 0.794 (p < 0.0001) (Figure 2). The MAF observed in the sample of cases was also well correlated with that of the sample of controls, with an R 2 of 0.819 (p < 0.0001) (Figure 2).

caption.p: Scatterplots showing the correlations between minor allele frequencies (MAF) for the 60 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) of interest.

sec.p: Overall results of analyses of the FMS phenotype are available in Table S1.

When the FMS phenotype was analysed according to allelic frequencies, a significant relationship was found for the SNPs rs1800629, rs11688000, and rs1800896, respectively related to the TNF, TACR1, and IL10 genes (Table 4). When, instead of testing the allelic frequencies, a genetic additive model was used, results were similar (respective p‐values: 0.034, 0.029 and 0.041; see Table S1). When, instead of testing the allelic frequencies, the genotype was used, a significant association was also found for TNF and TACR1, but no longer for IL10 (Table 5).

fn.p: Note: For each association found significant (p < 0.05), are shown, from left to right: the related gene and chromosome code (CHR), the single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) identifier, the minor (A1) and the major (A2) alleles, the expected minor allele frequency (MAF) for European (EU) databases, the observed MAF in the study samples, the odds ratio (OR) along with its 95% confidence interval limits, the standard error (SE), and the p‐value for the association test.

caption.p: Relationships between genotype, FMS and controls.

fn.p: Note: For each single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) for which a significant association with the clinical condition (FMS = fibromyalgia syndrome, or healthy matched controls) was found in the non‐adjusted analyses, this contingency table shows the respective distribution of the genotypes. Each genotype is the possible combination of the two alleles of the SNP, the minor and the major one. The statistical test was either the Fisher's exact test or the chi‐square test.
A post hoc analysis merged the ‘AA’ and the ‘AG’ genotypes, and the proportion of ‘GG’ genotypes was significantly superior in FMS patients than in controls (p = 0.012).
A post hoc analysis merged the ‘GG’ and the ‘GA’ genotypes, and the proportion of ‘AA’ genotypes was significantly superior in FMS patients than in controls (p = 0.012).

sec.p: When the analyses were adjusted on the NFR threshold as a covariate, a significant relationship was found for four SNPs. These were: the above mentioned rs11688000 (TACR1, OR = 0.502 [0.257–0.978]; p = 0.043); rs6295 (HTR1A, OR = 0.471 [0.244–0.910]; p = 0.025); rs11127292 (MYT1L, OR = 0.338 [0.122–0.936]; p = 0.037); and rs324420 (FAAH, OR = 2.81 [1.007–7.887]; p = 0.048). However, when the NFR threshold was compared between the different allelic categories of those four SNPs, no significant association was found (Figure 3). Similarly, when the genetic additive model was used to explain the NFR threshold within the sample of FMS patients, no relationship was found with any of the tested SNPs (Table S2).

caption.p: Box plots showing the distribution of the threshold of the nociceptive flexion reflex (NFR) depending on the genotype, for each of the four single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNP) for which a significant association has been found between the phenotype and the allelic frequency, after adjustment on the NFR threshold. No significant difference has been found between genotypes for any of the SNP studied.

sec.title: Other Associations
sec.p: We failed to determine the NFR threshold in some participants and as concomitant medications (e.g., analgesics, antidepressants or antiepileptics for pain) could contribute to this result, we performed an analysis of interaction and did not find any bias (Table S3).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study confirms central sensitisation in well‐defined cases of FMS, using NFR as an indirect proxy of CS and as a covariate in our genetic association study. Indeed, questionnaires (including BPI and FIQ) showed that the included FMS patients were substantially affected by their condition.

Our patients sample appears to display increased CS, as shown by the lower values of NFR threshold. Considering the NFR threshold a marker of spinal cord hyperexcitability (Lim et al. 2011) and that the threshold of the RIII reflex seems to be a reliable readout of spinal changes (Leone et al. 2025), our results (34 [24–46] vs. 58 [44–77] mA p = 0.0003) align with a recent meta‐analysis reporting—despite heterogeneity—a mean difference of −3.16 mA (95% CI limits: −6.82 and 0.50 mA) in NFR threshold between FMS patients and controls (Amiri et al. 2021). Most other case–control studies reported similar lower NFR thresholds in FMS (Gloor et al. 2022; Van Oosterwijck et al. 2025), alike in other chronic pain conditions (Mattoo et al. 2018).

Other findings highlight hyperexcitability of nociceptive systems, as previously reported (Gyorfi et al. 2022; Latremoliere and Woolf 2009). Pain intensity during NFR determination was higher in FMS patients, confirming previous studies (Desmeules et al. 2003, 2014). The higher TS ratio and CSI scores also supported increased CS (Neblett et al. 2013) even if for CSI the reliability of the results in FMS patients should be taken with caution because many items of the CSI overlap with those of the diagnostic criteria for FMS (Leone et al. 2025).

Genetic analyses of the selected candidate genes identified significant associations between the FMS phenotype and the allelic frequencies of rs1800629, rs11688000 and rs1800896, all previously described in pain or FMS comorbidities.

rs1800629 belongs to the TNF gene which encodes for tumour necrosis factor, a pro‐inflammatory chemical messenger produced by the immune system. In our observation, the MAF (A is the minor allele) was lower in cases (OR = 0.502), but it was the GG genotype that we found to be more frequent in FMS cases vs. controls. This is discrepant with previous reports, as an association study in somatoform disorder reported that GG appeared as protective towards pain, while being a carrier of the A allele might be a risk factor for developing somatoform disorder associated with pain (Harms et al. 2013). Furthermore, other studies reported higher serum concentrations of TNF in FMS patients (O'Mahony et al. 2021), and showed that the AA genotype was associated with a higher serum concentration of TNF (Wilson et al. 1997). However, we could not validate this underlying hypothesis, not having conducted assays on cytokines serum concentrations. Of note, rs1800629 had primarily been studied in migraine and rheumatoid arthritis, conditions which can be concomitant with FMS (O'Rielly et al. 2009; Schürks et al. 2009), and the AA genotype of rs1800629 has been found associated with sleep disorders and fatigue in cancer patients (Aouizerat et al. 2009). It remains possible that other SNPs than rs1800629 in connection with TNF are modulated in patients with FMS, given its influence on neural networks for the modulation of pain signalling (Baral et al. 2019). Finally, results could be explained by a type‐I error inflation with a loss of significance after adjustment on the NFR threshold.

rs11688000 belongs to TACR1 which encodes for a postsynaptic tachykinin receptor involved in CS (possibly in inflammatory conditions), and some polymorphisms of TACR1 may be involved in inter‐individual variability of pain (Storm et al. 2013). In our observation, the MAF (G is the minor allele) was lower in cases (OR = 0.576), but it was the GA genotype that we found to be more frequent in FMS cases. In a study in osteoarthritis (OA), the G allele at rs11688000 was associated with a decreased risk of symptomatic OA compared to asymptomatic OA cases, but the mechanism of pain is different in OA from FM (Warner et al. 2017). A study conducted in a smaller sample of Jewish patients failed to identify a significant association between FMS and polymorphisms of TACR1 (Ablin et al. 2009).

rs1800896 belongs to a gene encoding for interleukin 10, a cytokine implicated in the regulation of substance P, the natural agonist of the above mentioned tachykinin receptor TACR1 (Wallace et al. 2001). In our observation, the MAF (C is the minor allele) was higher in cases (OR = 1.684), but no genotype was found to be differently frequent in FMS cases. Testing this SNP was motivated by a recent systematic review reporting higher circulating concentrations of IL‐10 in FMS patients (O'Mahony et al. 2021) and rs1800896 has also been explored in other chronic pain conditions such as ankylosing spondylitis (Braga et al. 2021) or in irritable bowel syndrome (Zhu et al. 2019). The complexity in the genomics of inflammatory processes lies in the pro−/anti‐inflammatory balance, which is much more complex than the function of a sole protein (Peck et al. 2020). This could also explain our discrepant observations between the TNF‐ and IL10‐related SNPs. Studies on cytokines and their implications in pain expression of FMS are still needed.

Concerning the analyses we conducted with the NFR threshold as a covariate, the results have to be interpreted cautiously, as no genotype within any of these 4 SNPs was shown to influence the NFR threshold. A study in healthy volunteers previously explored the links between genotypic variations of rs6295 (HTR1A) and thermal perception levels as well as NFR thresholds, but like in our study, the authors did not observe any difference regarding NFR thresholds (Lindstedt et al. 2012). Results of rs11688000 (TACR1) can be interpreted as a non‐interaction of CS on the association with FMS, while those for rs324420 (FAAH), rs6295 (HTR1A) and rs11127292 (MYT1L) seem to be rather more a random effect than a true interaction of CS.

While we selected and studied a number of SNPs we considered to be of interest in the study of FMS, we did not identify significant associations. This applies to rs4796604 (HAP1), which has been identified as potentially associated with NFR threshold (Gloor et al. 2022), and recently a mechanistic study suggests that HAP1 may regulate Cav1.2 surface expression, which can lead to reduced neuronal excitability, BDNF secretion, inflammatory responses, and ultimately influences neuropathic pain progression (Pan et al. 2023). In our observation, we did not find significant association for the SNP related to HAP1 gene. Furthermore, our exploratory methods were strictly the same as in this previous study, although a difference was in the diagnosis criteria of FMS (ACR 2016 vs. 1990), and more concomitant medications that could influence NFR—even though we did not observe any influence of these treatments in our study (data not presented).

Other associations we did not confirm must also be discussed, including the one with rs3783641, though reported as associated with decreased pain sensitivity in FMS (Kim et al. 2013), and related to GCH1, a gene involved both in CS and in dopamine synthesis. This also applies to rs1799971 (OPRM1), previously described as associated with alterations of fMRI brain connectivity during pressure pain test in FMS (Ellerbrock et al. 2021). More noticeably, SNPs related to COMT had been reported to be linked to the risk of developing FMS (Estévez‐López et al. 2018; Matsuda et al. 2010) or to correlate with severe pain intensity in sensitised FMS patients (Gerra et al. 2024)—although results similar to ours have also been published (Lee et al. 2015). COMT has probably been the most studied gene in association studies of FMS, with mostly positive results, but meta‐analyses have generally either minimised the size of such association, or even rejected it (Tammimäki and Männistö 2012; Zhang et al. 2014; Lee et al. 2015). In addition, COMT variants have been suspected in the pathogenesis of pain, (e.g., experimental, back pain, migraine…). Furthermore, the natural frequency of the favouring genotype is quite high, and COMT might be rather involved in gene–gene or gene–environment interactions, which are however rarely studied. Finally, we failed to replicate the associations found for two SNPs by a GWAS of CWP patients, but the related genes (RNF123 and APT2C1) have no known involvement in pain or inflammation.

Discrepancies from other studies may stem from FMS heterogeneity as some phenotypes may be present involving biopsychosocial factors, psychological disturbances, and autonomic dysfunction that are difficult to address by cross‐sectional studies. Furthermore, if there is some genetic influence on both pain and its biopsychosocial determinants, this is unlikely to be monogenic, gene–gene, or polygenic risk scores. Moreover, gene–environment interactions are hardly addressed by gene‐association studies, even by GWAS.

Technical limitations include failed NFR measurements in some participants, although the team which conducted explorations had been trained for this purpose. Concomitant medications (e.g., antidepressants, analgesics, or anxiolytics) may have contributed, though our analysis found no bias. Stress, sleep quality, physical activity, or cardiac variability were not assessed (Sandrini et al. 2005). Failure rate was quite higher than we expected, considering the previous reports, although such rate had not been clearly reported in most studies (Amiri et al. 2021). However, some works reported 7/160 patients (4.4%) (Desmeules et al. 2014) or 8/15 patients (53%) (Umeda et al. 2013) with missing values.

In conclusion, we confirmed that spinal nociceptive excitability using NFR is higher in FMS patients and although several polymorphisms stand out in the analysis, no relationship between CS and the 60 selected SNPs could be highlighted. Larger cohort studies conducted in better phenotyped FMS patients, combined with either candidate gene association studies or GWAS, are still needed to complete our knowledge.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: This study was designed by N.M., G.P., B.P., V.L., C.D., J.D. and Y.G. The experiments were performed by N.M., V.L., G.P. and C.D. Genetic assays were performed by Y.G. and N.M. The data were analysed by B.P., Y.G., Y.R., C.D., M.D. and N.M. and the results were critically examined by all authors. N.M. had a primary role in preparing the manuscript, which was edited by G.P., J.D., Y.G. and C.D. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript and agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work.

sec.p: This work was supported by APICIL foundation.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

caption.p: Table S1: Overall results of the analyses of the association between the genotype and the phenotype (FMS = fibromyalgia syndrome, or healthy matched controls) for each of the 60 studied candidate genes. For each analysis are shown, from left to right: the single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) identifier, the related gene, the minor and the major alleles, the expected minor allele frequency (MAF) for European databases (as reported by https://www.ensembl.org/index.html), the observed MAF in the study samples, and the results of the simple comparison of allelic frequencies, the simple logistic regression in which a genetic additive model was used to express the genotype, and the same logistic regression with the NFR threshold taken as a covariate (adjusted analysis). To help to compare the results between each step, these are expressed as an odds ratio (OR) along with its 95% confidence interval limits, and the p‐value for the chi‐square test (conducted on the contingency table for the comparison of allelic frequencies, and on the Wald chi‐square test for the logistic regressions).

Table S2: Overall results of the analyses of the association between the genotype (expressed as a score from the genetic additive model) and the NFR threshold within the sample of fibromyalgia syndrome (FMS) patients. For each analysis are shown, from left to right: the single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) identifier, the related gene, the beta coefficient of the simple linear regression along with its 95% confidence interval limits, and the p‐value from the ANOVA that tested the significance of the model.

Table S3: Overall results of the analyses of the association between the NFR measurement type (failure for early pain, successful measurement, failure for excessive threshold) and the long‐term concomitant medications (not taken on the day of the visit) within the sample of fibromyalgia syndrome (FMS) patients.
